2.2: Checking species parameterisation for seabream

FAO Climate Change Risks to Fed Mariculture

Author

Tormey Reimer

Published

12 June 2026

← Previous: Check species: European seabass

Next: Check species: Drum/Croaker →

Overview

These documents runs a quick sanity check on the seabream model. For each model, we:

  1. Generate a default one-year sinusoidal temperature time-series centred on the species’ optimal feeding temperature.
  2. Load the species-specific bioenergetic parameters from data/raw_data/parameters_species.xlsx.
  3. Build a typical diet for the species from data/raw_data/feed_profiles_and_composition.xlsx.
  4. Simulate growth of a single representative individual using fish_growth().
  5. Plot all output variables as line graphs facetted by output measure to confirm that the model is behaving sensibly.
  6. Plot applicable parameters against some available data.
  7. If necessary, make some parameter changes to better fit the data.

Default feed parameters are used throughout (a generic whole-feed composition with reasonable digestibilities) since the purpose here is to check that the bioenergetic parameters produce plausible growth trajectories, not to evaluate any particular feed formulation.

Species parameters

Load species parameters for seabream 
this_species <- "seabream"

parameters_species <- read_excel(
  file.path(rawdata_path, 'parameters_species.xlsx'),
  sheet = this_species,
  trim_ws = T,
  n_max = 28
) %>%
  clean_names() %>% 
  select(used_in_script, description, value, unit, reference)

species_params <- parameters_species %>% pull(value) %>% as.numeric()
names(species_params) <- parameters_species %>% pull(used_in_script)

species_params[["overFmean"]] <- 0.025
species_params[["overFdelta"]] <- 0.0025

# Any missing?
species_params[is.na(species_params)]
doy FCR 
 NA  NA

Environmental/feed parameters

Load feed and ingredient data and calculate feed composition parameters 
feeds_df <- read_excel(here("data", "raw_data", "feed_profiles_and_composition.xlsx"), sheet = "all_feeds") %>%
  select(feed, ingredient, proportion) %>% 
  filter(feed == "European seabass 1")

ingredients_df <- read_excel(here("data", "raw_data", "all_ingredients.xlsx"), sheet = "all_ingredients") %>% 
  select(ingredient, protein, lipid, carb)

digestibility_df <- read_excel(here("data", "raw_data", "all_ingredients.xlsx"), sheet = "all_ingredients") %>% 
  clean_names() %>% 
  rename(
    protein_digestibility = mean_protein_digestibility, 
    lipid_digestibility = mean_lipid_digestibility, 
    carb_digestibility = mean_carb_digestibility
  ) %>% 
  select(ingredient, protein_digestibility, lipid_digestibility, carb_digestibility)

feed_params <- sum_feeds(
  format_feeds(feeds_df, ingredients_df, digestibility_df)
  )[[1]]

The temperature timeseries used is based on the mean current temperatures at farms assigned to this species in 04_assigning_locations.

Define simulation times and temperature timeseries 
# Get current mean temperatures for farms assigned to this model
farm_temps_all <- file.path(prepdata_path, "farm_IDs_meanyear_assigned", "farm_temps_meanyear_seabream.qs") %>% 
  qd_read() %>% 
  group_by(doy) %>% 
  reframe(sst = mean(sst))

farm_temps_all <- rbind(
  farm_temps_all,
  farm_temps_all %>% mutate(doy = doy + 365),
  farm_temps_all %>% mutate(doy = doy + 730)
)

# Get feeding response at each DOY
farm_temps_all$fr <- sapply(
    X = farm_temps_all$sst, 
    FUN = feeding_rate, 
    species_params = species_params
  )
farm_temps_all$fr[is.na(farm_temps_all$fr)] <- 0

# Get a good growing window
grow_days <- species_params[["gd"]] %>% as.integer()
gds <- farm_temps_all %>% 
  mutate(
    roll_fr = slide_dbl(fr, sum, .before = 0, .after = grow_days - 1)
  ) %>%
  slice(1:(nrow(farm_temps_all) - grow_days + 1)) %>%
  slice_max(roll_fr, n = 1) %>%
  mutate(end_doy = doy + grow_days - 1)

times <- c(
  t_start = gds$doy[1],
  t_end   = gds$end_doy[1],
  dt      = 1L
)

farm_temps <- farm_temps_all %>% 
  filter(doy >= times['t_start'] & doy <= times['t_end'])

Run checks

Each section below runs the sanity check. Plots show all 30 model output variables facetted by measure; the last row (harvest day) has NA values for all variables except weight, which is expected.

Check that all parameters being passed to the model functions are correct 
check_fish_growth_inputs(
  species_params = species_params,
  water_temp     = farm_temps$sst,
  feed_params    = feed_params,
  times          = c(
    "t_start" = min(farm_temps$doy),
    "t_end" = max(farm_temps$doy),
    "dt" = 1
  ),
  init_weight    = species_params['meanW'],
  ingmax         = species_params['meanImax']
)
All inputs look good — `fish_growth()` should run without errors.
Run fish_growth() for a representative individual 
set.seed(42)
one_fish <- fish_growth(
  species_params = species_params,
  water_temp     = farm_temps$sst,
  feed_params    = feed_params,
  times          = c(
    "t_start" = min(farm_temps$doy),
    "t_end" = max(farm_temps$doy),
    "dt" = 1
  ),
  init_weight    = species_params['meanW'],
  ingmax         = species_params['meanImax']
)

cat(sprintf(
  'Initial weight: %.1f g  |  Final weight: %.1f g (%.1f g)  |  Approx. FCR: %.2f |  Final SGR: %.1f \n',
  one_fish[1, 'weight'],
  tail(one_fish[, 'weight'], 1), species_params['CS'],
  sum(one_fish[, 'food_prov'], na.rm = T) / (tail(one_fish[, 'weight'], 1) - one_fish[1, 'weight']),
  tail(one_fish[, 'SGR'], 1)
))
Initial weight: 3.5 g  |  Final weight: 338.2 g (400.0 g)  |  Approx. FCR: 1.55 |  Final SGR: 0.6

Figure 1 shows all output variables over the grow-out period.

Sensitivity

Set uncertainty of parameters (and remove parameters we’re not testing the sensitivity of) 
# "flex" refers to the flexibility I'm willing to give each parameter (ie uncertainty)
flex_params <- tribble(
  ~param,     ~flex,
  "alpha",    2,
  "pk",       2,
  "k0",       2,
  "m",        2,
  "n",        2,
  "omega",    1,
  "a",        2,
  "k",        2,
  "epsO2",    2,
  "betac",    2,
  "meanW",    2,
  "meanImax", 3,
  "gd",       4,
  "epsprot",  4,
  "epslip",   4,
  "epscarb",  4
) %>% 
  arrange(flex)

This section looks at parameter sensitivity (how much the model responds to changes in each parameter) and parameter uncertainty (how flexible I am willing to be on each parameter in the calibration process). The output variables for which we’re going to assess sensitivity are weight and SGR.

Calculate parameter sensitivity for weight and SGR across ±10% parameter perturbations 
species_params_sens <- map(
  flex_params$param,
  function(nm) {
    map(c(0.9, 1.0, 1.1), function(level) {
      species_params[nm] <- species_params[nm] * level
      species_params
    }) %>% 
      setNames(c("low", "med", "high"))
  }
) %>% 
  setNames(flex_params$param)

set.seed(42)
sens_results <- map2_dfr(
  species_params_sens, 
  names(species_params_sens),
  function(sp, nm) {
    map_dfr(sp, function(sp_lev) {
      fish_growth(
        species_params = sp_lev,
        water_temp     = farm_temps$sst,
        feed_params    = feed_params,
        times          = c(
          "t_start" = min(farm_temps$doy),
          "t_end" = max(farm_temps$doy),
          "dt" = 1
        ),
        init_weight    = sp_lev['meanW'],
        ingmax         = sp_lev['meanImax']
      ) %>% 
        as.data.frame() %>% 
        tail(1) %>% 
        select(weight, SGR)
    }) %>% 
      mutate(
        param = nm, 
        level = names(sp)
      )
  }
) 

# Calculate sensitivity
sens_results <- sens_results %>%
  group_by(param) %>%
  summarise(across(c(weight, SGR), ~ (.x[level == "high"] - .x[level == "low"]) / (.x[level == "med"] * 0.2), .names = "{.col}")) %>% 
  pivot_longer(
    cols = !contains("param"),
    values_to = "sens", names_to = "output",
    names_transform = c(output = as.factor)
  ) %>% 
  left_join(flex_params, by = "param") %>% 
  mutate(param = factor(param, levels = rev(flex_params$param)))
Plot parameter sensitivity as a barplot facetted by output variable 
sens_results %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = param, y = sens, fill = output)) +
  geom_col(colour = "black") +
  coord_flip() +
  facet_wrap(~output) +
  prettyplot()

Hypercube adjustment

The hypercube adjustment uses Latin Hypercube Sampling (LHS) to search for parameter combinations that bring model output closer to observed data. Sampling ranges for each parameter are set inversely proportional to both its sensitivity (from Section 5) and its flexibility rank — so parameters that have a large effect on outputs or that we are less willing to change are given narrower sampling ranges (Figure 3).

The flexibility rank is set manually (subjectively) based on how confident I am with the values provided.

  • Minimum, maximum and optimum temperature parameters are not subject to change, since the fish are here grown at optimum temperature (and because temperature tolerance limits are generally available from the literature).
  • Commercial size is not subject to change. Growout days is flexible since estimates are subject to temperature.
  • Parameters taken from defaults or other species are the most flexible, as they’re really just “placeholder” values.
  • The derived parameters relating to internal model workings (e.g. alpha, m, n, and betac) are pretty flexible, as they are more a “means to an end” than important in and of themselves.
  • Parameters that are data-driven and measurable (e.g. meanW, meanImax) are less flexible, but still changeable.
  • All parameters are restricted to changes $$10% of their default value, to prevent wild adjustments.
Calculate per-parameter sampling ranges from sensitivity and flexibility 
# Combine sensitivity across both output variables (mean absolute sensitivity)
sens_combined <- sens_results %>%
  group_by(param, flex) %>%
  summarise(mean_abs_sens = mean(abs(sens)), .groups = "drop")

# Range score: inversely proportional to mean absolute sensitivity and flex rank.
# Cap 1/sens to avoid blow-up when sensitivity is near zero.
max_inv_sens <- 20
min_inv_sens <- 0.1
sens_combined <- sens_combined %>%
  mutate(
    inv_sens  = pmax(min_inv_sens, pmin(1 / pmax(mean_abs_sens, 1 / max_inv_sens), max_inv_sens)),
    raw_range = inv_sens * (1 / flex)
  )

# Cap range changes at +-10%
sens_combined <- sens_combined %>%
  mutate(
    param        = as.character(param),
    sample_range = scales::rescale(raw_range, to = c(0.005, 0.10))
  )
Plot sampling ranges per parameter 
sens_combined %>%
  mutate(param = factor(param, levels = param[order(sample_range)])) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = param, y = sample_range * 100)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#2171b5", colour = "black", width = 0.7) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(x = "Parameter", y = "Sampling range (+-% of baseline)") +
  theme_classic()

I’ve sourced target data from the following growth studies:

  • Hernández et al. (2003), all 10 of their samples have been used. They did not provide full temperature timeseries for the data, but the temperature ranges were fairly narrow (18-23\(^\circ\))
Get data from Hernandez et al. (2003) 
hernandez <- file.path(rawdata_path, "published_papers", "Hernandez et al. 2003", "Fig 4.csv") %>% 
  read.csv() %>% 
  clean_names()
colnames(hernandez) <- c("S1_x", "S1_y", "S2_x", "S2_y", "S3_x", "S3_y", "S4_x", "S4_y", "S5_x", "S5_y", "S6_x", "S6_y", "S7_x", "S7_y", "S8_x", "S8_y", "S9_x", "S9_y", "S10_x", "S10_y")

hernandez <- hernandez %>% 
  filter(S1_x != "X") %>% 
  mutate(point = c(1,2)) %>% 
  pivot_longer(
    cols = all_of(colnames(hernandez)), 
    names_to = c("sample", "dim"), names_sep = "_", 
    values_to = "value"
    ) %>% 
  pivot_wider(names_from = dim, values_from = value, id_cols = c(sample, point)) %>% 
  mutate(day = as.integer(as.numeric(x) * 30.345), weight = as.numeric(y)) %>% 
  group_by(sample) %>% 
  mutate(start_weight = min(weight), start_day = min(day), weight_gain = max(weight)-start_weight, grow_days = max(day) - start_day) %>% 
  ungroup() %>% 
  distinct(sample, start_weight, weight_gain, grow_days)
Define target experimental data as a list of experiments, each with its own temperature timeseries and named target outputs 
# Each entry in `target_experiments` represents one experimental condition.
# `water_temp` : data.frame with columns `day` (integer) and `temp_C` (numeric)
#                matching that experiment's temperature conditions.
# `targets`    : a named numeric vector of any model output columns to fit.
#                Different experiments can supply different (or overlapping) outputs.

# SS fitness is computed as the MEAN squared error across all (experiment × output)
# observations, so experiments with more targets do not dominate the fit.
target_experiments <- list(
  list(
    # Defaults
    water_temp = farm_temps$sst,
    targets    = c(weight_change = unname(species_params["CS"] - species_params["meanW"])),
    meanW      = species_params["meanW"]
  ),
  list(
    # Hernandez et al. (2003) sample 1
    water_temp = rep(mean(c(18,23)), hernandez$grow_days[hernandez$sample == "S1"]),
    targets    = c(weight_change = hernandez$weight_gain[hernandez$sample == "S1"]),
    meanW      = hernandez$start_weight[hernandez$sample == "S1"] 
  ),
  list(
    # Hernandez et al. (2003) sample 2
    water_temp = rep(mean(c(18,23)), hernandez$grow_days[hernandez$sample == "S2"]),
    targets    = c(weight_change = hernandez$weight_gain[hernandez$sample == "S2"]),
    meanW      = hernandez$start_weight[hernandez$sample == "S2"] 
  ),
  list(
    # Hernandez et al. (2003) sample 3
    water_temp = rep(mean(c(18,23)), hernandez$grow_days[hernandez$sample == "S3"]),
    targets    = c(weight_change = hernandez$weight_gain[hernandez$sample == "S3"]),
    meanW      = hernandez$start_weight[hernandez$sample == "S3"] 
  ),
  list(
    # Hernandez et al. (2003) sample 4
    water_temp = rep(mean(c(18,23)), hernandez$grow_days[hernandez$sample == "S4"]),
    targets    = c(weight_change = hernandez$weight_gain[hernandez$sample == "S4"]),
    meanW      = hernandez$start_weight[hernandez$sample == "S4"] 
  ),
  list(
    # Hernandez et al. (2003) sample 5
    water_temp = rep(mean(c(18,23)), hernandez$grow_days[hernandez$sample == "S5"]),
    targets    = c(weight_change = hernandez$weight_gain[hernandez$sample == "S5"]),
    meanW      = hernandez$start_weight[hernandez$sample == "S5"] 
  ),
  list(
    # Hernandez et al. (2003) sample 6
    water_temp = rep(mean(c(18,23)), hernandez$grow_days[hernandez$sample == "S6"]),
    targets    = c(weight_change = hernandez$weight_gain[hernandez$sample == "S6"]),
    meanW      = hernandez$start_weight[hernandez$sample == "S6"] 
  ),
  list(
    # Hernandez et al. (2003) sample 7
    water_temp = rep(mean(c(18,23)), hernandez$grow_days[hernandez$sample == "S7"]),
    targets    = c(weight_change = hernandez$weight_gain[hernandez$sample == "S7"]),
    meanW      = hernandez$start_weight[hernandez$sample == "S7"] 
  ),
  list(
    # Hernandez et al. (2003) sample 8
    water_temp = rep(mean(c(18,23)), hernandez$grow_days[hernandez$sample == "S8"]),
    targets    = c(weight_change = hernandez$weight_gain[hernandez$sample == "S8"]),
    meanW      = hernandez$start_weight[hernandez$sample == "S8"] 
  ),
  list(
    # Hernandez et al. (2003) sample 9
    water_temp = rep(mean(c(18,23)), hernandez$grow_days[hernandez$sample == "S9"]),
    targets    = c(weight_change = hernandez$weight_gain[hernandez$sample == "S9"]),
    meanW      = hernandez$start_weight[hernandez$sample == "S9"] 
  ),
  list(
    # Hernandez et al. (2003) sample 10
    water_temp = rep(mean(c(18,23)), hernandez$grow_days[hernandez$sample == "S10"]),
    targets    = c(weight_change = hernandez$weight_gain[hernandez$sample == "S10"]),
    meanW      = hernandez$start_weight[hernandez$sample == "S10"] 
  )
)

n_pairs <- length(target_experiments)

# This function processes the fish_growth() output to get it ready for comparison. It's defined here so that if different comparisons are added in this chunk I can change the calculation everywhere at once
get_comparison_stats <- function(df) {
  dfdf <- as.data.frame(df)
  data.frame(
    weight_change = tail(dfdf$weight, 1) - head(dfdf$weight, 1),
    SGR = tail(dfdf$SGR, 1)
  )
}
Generate LHS parameter samples and run model for each (1000 samples × n_pairs, with progress updates every 100) 
n_samples     <- 2500
param_names   <- sens_combined$param
sample_ranges <- sens_combined$sample_range
n_params      <- length(param_names)

set.seed(42)
lhs_raw <- randomLHS(n_samples, n_params)

# Map [0, 1] to [1 - range, 1 + range] multipliers for each parameter
multiplier_matrix <- sweep(lhs_raw * 2 - 1, 2, sample_ranges, "*") + 1
colnames(multiplier_matrix) <- param_names

# Run model once per experiment for each LHS sample.
# All final-row model output columns are retained so any named target can be used.
lhs_results <- vector("list", n_samples)
start_time  <- proc.time()["elapsed"]

for (i in seq_len(n_samples)) {
  if (i %% 500 == 0) {
    elapsed <- round(proc.time()["elapsed"] - start_time, 1)
    rate    <- i / elapsed
    eta     <- round((n_samples - i) / rate, 1)
    cat(sprintf(
      "Sample %4d / %d | elapsed: %.0fs | ETA: %.0fs\n",
      i, n_samples, elapsed, eta
    ))
  }

  sp <- species_params
  for (j in param_names) {
    sp[j] <- species_params[j] * multiplier_matrix[i, j]
  }

  # Run once per experiment; keep all output columns from the final timestep
  pair_results <- vector("list", n_pairs)
  for (k in seq_len(n_pairs)) {
    wt <- target_experiments[[k]]$water_temp

    change_params <- names(target_experiments[[k]])[names(target_experiments[[k]]) %in% names(species_params)]
    if (length(change_params) > 0) {
      for (c in change_params) {
        sp[c] <- unlist(target_experiments[[k]][c])
      }      
    }

    result <- tryCatch(
      fish_growth(
        species_params = sp,
        water_temp     = wt,
        feed_params    = feed_params,
        times          = c(
          "t_start" = 1,
          "t_end"   = length(wt),
          "dt"      = 1
        ),
        init_weight = sp["meanW"],
        ingmax      = sp["meanImax"]
      ),
      error = function(e) NULL
    )
    if (!is.null(result)) {
      final <- get_comparison_stats(result)
      pair_results[[k]] <- final %>% mutate(sample = i, pair = k, .before = 1)
    }
  }

  pair_df <- bind_rows(pair_results)
  # Only retain samples where all experiments completed successfully
  if (nrow(pair_df) == n_pairs) {
    lhs_results[[i]] <- pair_df
  }
}
Sample  500 / 2500 | elapsed: 136s | ETA: 543s
Sample 1000 / 2500 | elapsed: 287s | ETA: 431s
Sample 1500 / 2500 | elapsed: 434s | ETA: 289s
Sample 2000 / 2500 | elapsed: 572s | ETA: 143s
Sample 2500 / 2500 | elapsed: 746s | ETA: 0s
Generate LHS parameter samples and run model for each (1000 samples × n_pairs, with progress updates every 100) 
cat(sprintf(
  "Done. Total time: %.1fs\n",
  proc.time()["elapsed"] - start_time
))
Done. Total time: 746.5s
Generate LHS parameter samples and run model for each (1000 samples × n_pairs, with progress updates every 100) 
# One row per (sample, experiment)
lhs_results_df <- bind_rows(lhs_results)
Calculate mean squared error across all (experiment × output) observations, matching each run to its experiment-specific targets 
# For each (sample, experiment) row, compute squared errors for each named target and collect them into a long table of individual squared errors.
compute_sq_errors <- function(results_df, experiments) {
  map_dfr(seq_len(nrow(results_df)), function(row_i) {
    row    <- results_df[row_i, ]
    k      <- row$pair
    tgts   <- experiments[[k]]$targets
    map_dfr(names(tgts), function(col) {
      modelled <- row[[col]]
      data.frame(
        sample   = row$sample,
        pair     = k,
        output   = col,
        sq_error = (modelled - tgts[[col]])^2
      )
    })
  })
}

sq_errors_df <- compute_sq_errors(lhs_results_df, target_experiments)

# Mean SS per sample: mean squared error across all (experiment × output) observations
# Weight change is valued over all other target types
lhs_ss_df <- sq_errors_df %>%
  group_by(output) %>% 
  mutate(sq_error = scales::rescale(sq_error)) %>% 
  ungroup() %>% 
  mutate(w = if_else(output == "weight_change", 2, 1)) %>%
  group_by(sample) %>%
  summarise(mean_SS = mean(sq_error), .groups = "drop")

# Attach multipliers for inspection of top-performing samples
multipliers_df <- as.data.frame(multiplier_matrix) %>%
  mutate(sample = seq_len(n_samples))

lhs_ss_df <- lhs_ss_df %>%
  left_join(multipliers_df, by = "sample")

# Baseline: run the unadjusted model once per experiment and compute mean SS
baseline_results_df <- map_dfr(seq_len(n_pairs), function(k) {
  wt <- target_experiments[[k]]$water_temp
  sp <- species_params
  if (!is.null(target_experiments[[k]]$initW)) {sp["meanW"] <- target_experiments[[k]]$initW}
  set.seed(42)
  bf <- fish_growth(
    species_params = sp,
    water_temp     = wt,
    feed_params    = feed_params,
    times          = c(
      "t_start" = 1,
      "t_end"   = length(wt),
      "dt"      = 1
    ),
    init_weight = sp["meanW"],
    ingmax      = sp["meanImax"]
  )
  
  get_comparison_stats(bf) %>% 
    mutate(sample = 0L, pair = k)
})

baseline_sq_errors <- compute_sq_errors(baseline_results_df, target_experiments)
baseline_mean_SS   <- baseline_sq_errors %>%
  group_by(output) %>% 
  mutate(sq_error = scales::rescale(sq_error)) %>% 
  ungroup() %>% 
  group_by(sample) %>%
  summarise(mean_SS = mean(sq_error), .groups = "drop") %>% 
  pull(mean_SS)

cat(sprintf(
  "Samples completed: %d / %d\n",
  n_distinct(lhs_ss_df$sample), n_samples
))
Samples completed: 2500 / 2500

Figure 4 shows the distribution of mean squared error scores across all samples.

Plot distribution of mean SS scores across all LHS samples 
lhs_ss_df %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = mean_SS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 50, fill = "#2171b5", colour = "black", linewidth = 0.2) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = baseline_mean_SS, colour = "red", linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(x = "Mean squared error", y = "Count") +
  theme_classic()

The parameter multipliers for the best-fitting sample (relative to baseline) are:

Show parameter multipliers for the best-fitting sample 
best_sample <- lhs_ss_df %>%
  arrange(mean_SS) %>%
  slice_head(n = 1) %>%
  pull(sample)

best_lhs_results <- lhs_ss_df %>%
  filter(sample == best_sample) %>%
  select(all_of(param_names)) %>%
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "param", values_to = "multiplier") %>%
  mutate(
    pct_change    = (multiplier - 1) * 100,
    baseline_val  = species_params[param],
    adjusted_val  = baseline_val * multiplier
  ) %>%
  arrange(desc(abs(pct_change)))

best_lhs_results %>%
  gt() %>%
  fmt_number(columns = c(multiplier, pct_change), decimals = 2) %>%
  fmt_number(columns = c(baseline_val, adjusted_val), decimals = 4)

Calibrated run

Run fish_growth() with the calibrated parameter values 
# Run with the default temperature timeseries for the trajectory plot (same as
# the initial checks above), giving a single representative growth trajectory.
set.seed(42)

times <- c(
  "t_start" = gds$doy[1],
  "t_end"   = gds$doy[1] + unname(species_params_new["gd"]) - 1,
  "dt"      = 1
)

farm_temps_new <- farm_temps_all %>% 
  filter(doy >= times['t_start'] & doy <= times['t_end'])

one_fish_new <- fish_growth(
  species_params = species_params_new,
  water_temp     = farm_temps_new$sst,
  feed_params    = feed_params,
  times          = times,
  init_weight    = species_params_new['meanW'],
  ingmax         = species_params_new['meanImax']
)

# Also validate against each paired experimental temperature series to confirm
# that the best parameter set improves fit across all observed conditions.
calibrated_pair_results <- map_dfr(seq_len(n_pairs), function(k) {
  wt <- target_experiments[[k]]$water_temp
  sp <- species_params_new
  if (!is.null(target_experiments[[k]]$initW)) {sp["meanW"] <- target_experiments[[k]]$initW}
  set.seed(42)
  result <- fish_growth(
    species_params = sp,
    water_temp     = wt,
    feed_params    = feed_params,
    times          = c(
      "t_start" = 1,
      "t_end"   = length(wt),
      "dt"      = 1
    ),
    init_weight = sp['meanW'],
    ingmax      = sp['meanImax']
  )
  get_comparison_stats(result) %>% mutate(pair = k)
})

# Summary comparison: calibrated vs. target per experiment
# Only columns that appear in each experiment's `targets` vector are shown.
calibrated_summary <- map_dfr(seq_len(n_pairs), function(k) {
  row  <- calibrated_pair_results %>% filter(pair == k)
  tgts <- target_experiments[[k]]$targets
  map_dfr(names(tgts), function(col) {
    data.frame(
      pair      = k,
      output    = col,
      modelled  = row[[col]],
      target    = tgts[[col]],
      diff      = row[[col]] - tgts[[col]]
    )
  })
})
Display modelled vs. target values for each experiment after calibration 
calibrated_summary %>%
  gt() %>%
  fmt_number(columns = c(modelled, target, diff), decimals = 2)

Figure 5 shows all output variables over the grow-out period.

Save calibrated species parameters 
qd_save(
  species_params_new, 
  file.path(prepdata_path, "species_parameters", "species_parameters_seabream.qs")
)

← Previous: Check species: European seabass

Next: Check species: Drum/Croaker →

References

Hernández, Juan M, Eucario Gasca-Leyva, Carmelo J León, and J. M Vergara. 2003. ‘A Growth Model for Gilthead Seabream (Sparus Aurata)’. Ecological Modelling 165 (2): 265–83. https://doi.org/10.1016/S0304-3800(03)00095-4.
